description: Use 4NT to ask for aces when considering slam
purpose: Determine whether the partnership has enough aces to bid slam safely

teaching.tradeoff: Using 4NT as Blackwood means you can't use it as a natural quantitative raise to slam.
teaching.principle: Before bidding slam, check for aces — missing two aces means the opponents can cash two tricks immediately.
commonMistakes: Don't bid Blackwood without an agreed trump fit — you need a fit before asking for aces
5NT king ask guarantees all four aces are accounted for — never bid 5NT missing an ace

explanationEntries.displayText: Hand has slam interest (enough combined strength for slam)
explanationEntries.contrastiveDisplayText: Not enough strength for slam exploration

explanationEntries.displayText: Hand has no void that would distort a plain-Blackwood ace ask
explanationEntries.contrastiveDisplayText: A void makes plain Blackwood a misleading ace ask

explanationEntries.displayText: Blackwood 4NT: asks partner how many aces they hold
5NT asks for kings (guarantees all aces are held)
Sign off at small slam in the agreed trump suit
Pass and accept partner's king reply when grand slam is not right
Bid grand slam in the agreed trump suit with all aces and kings
Stop at 5-level — partnership is missing too many aces for slam

clauses.rationale: plain Blackwood should not be used with a void

teachingLabel.name: Blackwood 4NT
teachingLabel.summary: Ask partner how many aces they hold to evaluate slam potential

teachingLabel.name: 5♣ — 0 (or 4) aces
teachingLabel.summary: Show zero aces in response to partner's Blackwood ask

teachingLabel.name: 5♣ — 4 aces
teachingLabel.summary: Show all four aces in response to partner's Blackwood ask

teachingLabel.name: 5♦ — 1 ace
teachingLabel.summary: Show exactly one ace in response to partner's Blackwood ask

teachingLabel.name: 5♥ — 2 aces
teachingLabel.summary: Show exactly two aces in response to partner's Blackwood ask

teachingLabel.name: 5♠ — 3 aces
teachingLabel.summary: Show exactly three aces in response to partner's Blackwood ask

clauses.description: All four aces accounted for between the partnership

teachingLabel.name: Ask for kings
teachingLabel.summary: Confirm all aces are held and ask partner how many kings they have

clauses.description: Partnership holds at least three aces

teachingLabel.name: Small slam in agreed suit
teachingLabel.summary: Bid small slam in the agreed trump suit after confirming sufficient aces

clauses.description: Partnership is missing at least two aces

teachingLabel.name: Sign off in agreed suit
teachingLabel.summary: Stop below slam in the agreed trump suit after learning the partnership is missing too many aces

teachingLabel.name: 6♣ — 0 kings
teachingLabel.summary: Show zero kings in response to partner's king ask

teachingLabel.name: 6♣ — 4 kings
teachingLabel.summary: Share the same 6♣ reply used for 0 or 4 kings in plain Blackwood

teachingLabel.name: 6♦ — 1 king
teachingLabel.summary: Show exactly one king in response to partner's king ask

teachingLabel.name: 6♥ — 2 kings
teachingLabel.summary: Show exactly two kings in response to partner's king ask

teachingLabel.name: 6♠ — 3 kings
teachingLabel.summary: Show exactly three kings in response to partner's king ask

clauses.description: Partnership is still short of all four kings

teachingLabel.name: Natural small slam
teachingLabel.summary: Bid the best natural small slam after the king reply when grand slam is not justified

clauses.description: Partnership is still short of all four kings

teachingLabel.name: Pass — no grand slam
teachingLabel.summary: Pass when partner's king reply is already the right natural small-slam contract

teachingLabel.name: Grand slam in agreed suit
teachingLabel.summary: Bid grand slam in the agreed trump suit after confirming all aces and sufficient kings

definitions.description: Hand has slam-level values (17+ HCP consistent with partnership slam values)
Hand has a void in some suit, making plain Blackwood misleading
Number of aces held in hand (0-4)
Number of kings held in hand (0-4)
Combined partnership aces (own + partner's disclosed response, 0-8)
Combined partnership kings (own + partner's disclosed response, 0-8)

summaryCard.trigger: A trump fit is agreed and you are exploring slam
summaryCard.partnership: Confirm whether you play classic Blackwood before using the ask. Most modern tournament partnerships play Roman Keycard Blackwood (RKC) rather than classic Blackwood; RKC counts the trump king as a fifth keycard. The ask still presumes a solid trump suit, because missing top trump honors can defeat slam even when every ace is accounted for.

whenToUse.gloss: Use the ace ask after a fit is agreed and the partnership still needs one control check before committing higher.
Use the king ask only after the ace reply still leaves grand slam in view.

workedAuctions.label: Ace ask reaches a safe small slam

calls.rationale: The partnership starts by agreeing hearts as trumps.
Responder confirms the heart fit and shows a hand interested in going higher.
Blackwood asks how many aces responder holds before the partnership commits to slam.
Responder shows two aces, so opener knows the partnership is not missing two fast losers.
With enough combined control, opener signs off in the small slam.

workedAuctions.label: King ask checks for the grand

calls.rationale: The partnership establishes spades as trumps.
Responder confirms the fit and keeps slam in the picture.
Opener starts with the ace ask.
The reply shows that the partnership has all the aces covered in classic Blackwood.
Now opener asks for kings because the ace reply kept grand slam alive.
Responder shows one king, letting opener judge whether to stop or stretch.
One king is enough for a practical small slam but not enough to guarantee the grand.

workedAuctions.label: Counter-example: a void hides a wasted control

calls.rationale: Opener starts with a natural spade opening.
Responder confirms the spade fit and shows slam-try strength.
Opener asks for aces, unable to describe the club void through Blackwood.
Responder shows two aces — but classic Blackwood cannot reveal which two.

Opener commits to slam trusting the ace count. Slam fails because partner's club ace faces the void and wins no extra trick, while a heart loser plus a diamond loser cash out. Cue-bidding would have exposed the duplicated control and steered the partnership to a safer contract.

workedAuctions.label: Counter-example: no fit makes the ask meaningless

calls.rationale: No trump suit has been agreed yet.
Without an agreed fit, this call is no longer a clean ace ask and may be read as a natural or quantitative move.
The partnership never gets the ace-count answer it wanted, because the auction never established the conditions that make Blackwood meaningful.

interference.reason: This reference covers uncontested slam auctions; once the opponents act, partnership-specific control methods usually take over.

items.note: Shared with all aces in classic Blackwood.
Shows one ace. Asker usually signs off, since a slam with two aces missing is off against an opening lead.
Shows two aces. Asker has enough control information to commit to small slam when the combined strength supports it.
Shows three aces. Asker may continue with the king ask when a grand slam is still in view.
Shared with no aces in classic Blackwood.

relatedLinks.discriminator: Agree the trump suit naturally before asking for aces; without a fit the reply has no clear meaning.
After a forcing start uncovers a fit, Blackwood becomes the later control check for slam decisions.
